speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Philip Asplund grew up on the family's preemption on Dry Ridge and worked most of his life in the woods including nearly 25 years as a horse teamster. Here he discusses his logging experience and reminisces about the home place, Big Anderson, and Troy as a trading center.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I'm getting better and better every day, and then he said, ha ha ha. He's got a character.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the trouble with him, why he needed these canes?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: He had them short that wouldn't heal on his place. The meat was all gone and nothing but the bone. We used to see him sit on the creek there and wash his feet, you know, but nothing but the bone. No meat on the left at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he lived by himself?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yeah. He saw his wood, of course the trees, the dry trees were right close to his place there. He sat there, sat on a little stool, you know, he used to go like this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that sawing him?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah. He didn't make much headway but he didn't eat much wood either, so. And I imagine he had a poor saw, he probably didn't know how to fix the saw either. We offered to, my brother and I, offered to saw, help him saw wood, but no, he wouldn't want my help. And if you brought food down to him, you know, he wouldn't eat it unless you ate with him, because he's afraid to be poisoned. He's all queer, are we? He always thought somebody wanted to poison him, you know. We got a funny guy back here, he is a crippled up now, so bad. A fat, fat one. John Fathman, shoe safe one. You've probably seen him around here. Sure, sure. Well he's a crippled up now, he can't walk. And so I went in there this morning to see how he was. He said, look in the other room. I said, what's the matter? What's in there?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: So I looked, then the blanket and the quilt were laying on the floor, like that in front of the bed. There was nothing but a mattress on the bed. He said, there's a woman in there. My God, I said, you're lucky if you got a woman in the house. He laughed. So I picked up the blanket, put them on the bed. I said, there's no woman here. How can they get in if you've got locked? So he must have been losing his mind. Yeah. They're going to take him away, I guess. The nurse was supposed to come down yesterday, but I don't think she come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you going to take him to the county home?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: To the nursing home, I guess. Yeah, in Moscow. Yeah. I wouldn't wonder, but he's had a stroke because he's had his arm involved with him too. And at one side, he can't walk now without hanging on to a chair.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This Big Anderson, was he supposed to have been a sailor? Did you hear that?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, I don't know. I don't remember that. He used to tell us a lot of stories, but he told us he used to live out in Big Berridge. He had a homestead there. Then he sold a homestead and bought one horse from my dad and then one horse from someplace else. And then he went to Canada and homesteaded over there. And then he come back again. Then he was telling us about the story when he crossed the Rocky Mountain that Cougar was after, fallen for miles. And pretty soon he attacked him, he said. So then he said he picked up sand and threw it at the Cougar and he quit. I don't know if he's telling a lie or not, but he had that kind of stories to tell us kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he scare the kids because he was so big?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, no, I don't think, well if they met him alone maybe they were, someone was scared, but we knew him because he lived close to our place. I imagine people that didn't know him probably got scared, but.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have, know how he managed to get by without working or anything?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well he had $500 in the bank here and he had that on interest. I don't know how much the interest was at that time, but that's all he lived on. So then when he went to town, well he'd buy a sack of flour and a pail of herring or some other kind of fish. That's all he bought. Then he made that kind of bread on top of that heater he had there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of heater was this?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: It was one of them with two lids on top, you know, you open the door and put it in, it's a heavy cast iron stove. And I say, I was down there a few years ago, they had been logging there and they made a, the shack was gone, but they made a road right across and I found pieces of his stove there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a special kind of bread that he liked or just a flat bread?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: He just mixed it up with water I think and cooked it on top of the stove, I think that's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that he made his own clothes too?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yeah, he made his own clothes. He made them out of denim, the overalls. They were wide, they were a lot wider than ordinary overalls. And the socks, that's all he had was burlap wrapped around his legs. And shoes, he made out of big, big wide shoes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he walked with, did he walk with usually one cane?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, he walked with two, he had only, I see only one there. But at that time, this was taken quite a while ago probably, he had two canes. He'd go like this, you know. Just hunched over and do it over again. But he walked a long ways I tell you. He'd go to walk the tour here. Of course, he'd, somebody'd pick him up always on the road, somebody'd come along. But he'd start, he'd walk. And then he'd go into the salon or somebody'd treat him, you know, and he was pretty stewed coming home. Then he'd talk to me and everything. He was raised pretty lively then. But I doubt if he spent any of the money of his own, I don't see how he could because he only had the $500 and that's what he lived on. Well if he could get by on the interest off the $500 he could, he really had something. Yeah, he used off the interest, you know, the interest wouldn't be much, $500. I wonder where he'd get the $500 from. Well, he sold his homestead out there on the ridge for it. It was good homestead there but of course the money, the land wasn't worth much then. Then he went to Canada and homesteaded and I don't know if he sold that or not. He must have. But probably didn't get that much out of that neither.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about his machine, his perpetual motion machine?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, he had it in the mind of a nobody ever got to see it. He wouldn't show it to anybody. But he'd always tell when he'd come up to our place that he'd soon, well that machine soon be going on. It'd be into money. He was going to get lots of money then. I soon got it now, so we'll run into this. But he never got it to go. Nobody ever got to see that thing because that was inside the mind. They had a door there. Sounds like him saying I'm getting feeling better and better all the time. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You could really see the bone on this?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yeah, oh sure, sure. There wasn't hardly any meat left on bone. And it smelled to high heaven. And the only thing he used was just water, quick water, wash. I don't think he ever had any medicine over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember when he died?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah. I don't mean the year but... Yeah, I don't remember that. They took him away. There was a bachelor that lived up on the hill back of Mackenzie's place. He took him. But he couldn't keep him so pretty soon he took him to the hospital and then he died. I imagine when they gave him a bath he died. That kills a lot of people, you know, they're not used to it. They cleaned him up, you know. That probably happened to this fellow here too. I don't think he had the bath that he moved in here. And he's got all this garbage. All over the place. He lived there pretty two months and he's got it all in boxes underneath the table. I was in the old O .K. Olsen place not too long ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, just looking it over inside there and he's got all the old magazines, the old salesman magazines, all that old stuff from the 1940s and 50s, it's all lying around. He had a lot of pictures down there, somebody stole them. Oh, that I didn't know about. You know, there was people living there after he moved out. Yeah, I heard that. Some of the hippies. Yeah. And so I guess they told the sheriffs about Moscow. The sheriffs went down there but them fellows told the sheriffs that they had permission to move in there and they wasn't going to move out. So pretty soon they said, this is just what I've heard. There was some more of the sheriffs come down to Chevron and they got them out because they weren't supposed to be there. Of course that's just what I hear to you. Yeah. Well, they're gone, that's for sure. Yeah, they're gone. There's one more thing about Big Anderson I'm wondering.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a hermit or was he a friendly guy?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, yes, he was pretty friendly. You could go there, he opened the door for you and let you in. He talked like everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He liked company then?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, I think so. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he laugh a lot?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, he'd tell stories, you know, and then he'd laugh. Yeah, he'd always have that ha ha ha ha.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know of where he came here from? I mean, did he come out here straight from Sweden?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, that I don't know. You see, these old sons, they were related to him. These old sons here, that's Russell's grandfather there. And then he has another brother here, Jonas Olson, that's another. They were cousins or something. So, he was telling that he bought a hack for this old son, a brand new one. And he promised him to go along to Canada, you see, and they were going to ride in that hack to Canada. But after he bought the hack for him, Olson wouldn't go. So, he didn't have nothing of no use for him anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Big Anderson didn't?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, no, that's what he told, you see. He didn't have no use for him anymore because he bought him that hack. And I guess that cost quite a bit at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Big Anderson bought the hack for him?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, because he promised to go along with Canada, they'd ride in that hack to Canada, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he known to be a strong man?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yeah, he must have been a powerful man in the young days. You know, he was religious too, and he read his Bible in the old glasses. And he never had any light in his house, no lamp. I'd heard that from somebody told me that he used to take big logs for his wood and haul them up to his place, just carry them. Well, I don't know if he would do that at that time. He wasn't very well at that time, he kind of had legs, otherwise he was powerful. You know, I wanted to ask you a little about how your folks came to locate out in this country. Well, when they come from Sweden, my mother comes from Norway. She don't know her folks at all. Her mother died when she was a little baby, and somebody took her and brought her to this country, to Minnesota. And there's where my father -in -law married her. So she don't know what to come of her father if she had more sisters or brothers in Norway or not. But my father come from Sweden to Minnesota, and then from there out here. And he worked for this Elsie Nelson's father, you know. He had this contract on this railroad over here to build a railroad from Moscow down to the ocean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The one that came out here through Troy and down the canyon?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah. So my father, while away, I'm going to show you, my father found a round stone just like an egg. I still got that. The best plan was settled already up here when he come, but he said he still could have got some there. But then as an Anderson's lived out here, there was three brothers settled out there around a close by Hooye, where I was born. And they wrote to him and told him, or I don't know if they wrote to him, but anyway they seen him. And they said, you can't live around the N .C. there's no timber. How are you going to get along without timber? You can't do nothing without timber. But then he went up here to Derry, down below Dalberg's place there, about a mile, and he homesteaded a place there. But I guess that's when Lena's Harrison got him to go down there, that's right. But there was somebody else told them they couldn't be out of the agency because no timber. Well, they said they'd better land on the south, farther south than the quarter was. So he gave that up, that hunk, and then took the other one on preemption. Paid so much in April.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And this was the place out on Drybridge?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Drybridge, yeah. Right below Mackenzie's. We had a house right on top of the canyon, back down below.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When he took the place there wasn't anything on it?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Not one foot of land. But a lot of these places, you know, there's meadows when you get up around Helmer and Bowell, there's a lot of meadows there. There was no timber on it. Never been on timber on meadows, I don't know why. But there was no meadowland out there where we were. Everything had to be cleared. Yeah, they cut the big pines down and rolled them together and burned them up. And most of the time they burnt the stumps off even the ground and they went over them. And that's the way it was until, most of them until they started with tractors and they had to get rid of them stumps in the ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it mostly big timber like that? Big timber and a lot of space room between them?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh, the biggest, nicest pine you ever saw in Tamarack. You bet. But there was no underbrush. No underbrush. You could drive any place where it wasn't too rough of ground, between the trees. The grasses, pine grass up, that's all. My mother told me about when they first got there, they just took the saw and cut hay around between the trees for the cow, you know, and horses. The Indians kept the fire to dry long, kept the brush down. Now the brush is a bed you can't walk through after a white man got over. The Indians burned it on purpose. They took care of it. They drove all over them. They mostly drove over the road hardback but some of them had themes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when your father took the preemption there, was there, did he have any neighbors around him or was he pretty much?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, there was neighbors. Lieners Anderson was right across the canyon. Right across the canyon, straight across from our place and August Anderson was right down at the bottom of the creek, just a quarter mile down there. And then there was another neighbor on the other side, Lieners Carlson. They were all, they could see what was going on there but they could even, if you hollered, you could hear him across the canyon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he start to build a place right away, a home?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, I guess he did. He built the lean -to up on that homestead up there. That's where my sister was born, the lean -to. So I guess he started building as soon as he'd get lumber, of course. And I guess they had sawmills here and there. I wonder about that construction of that railroad out through here. I would think that must have given a lot of jobs to people when that came through. Oh yeah. Well, they had to, my dad worked out, you know. My dad and my mother had to do most of the work, the hay and everything because he had to be out working. Because there was no income on the farm, you know, for the family. He cared, he told he cared 50 pounds of flour from Moscow on his back. It's a long way, it's nine miles from here to Troy and tell at 12 through Moscow. So, I imagine that got pretty heavy before he got home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was before there was it, Troy?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, oh yeah. Before Volmer. This was Volmer. I remember when we used to say we were going to Volmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, where would he work out?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, he worked on the railroad and then in the harvest, the Genesee, you know, that. They got quite a bit of cultivation there almost right away because that wasn't all in the timber there. There wasn't hardly any timber there, it was all sawed, I guess. They broke up and they soon got fields there. So, they went out there and helped in the harvest. Them days they took quite a few men around the harvest around the trash machine. Did you ever see a horse power thrasher machine? Just the pictures, never seen it. Yeah, that's why I got one, but it didn't in very good shape. I remember too. I remember when they tried. They had to haul lumber, haul the water in the barrel to keep the water down for the horses going around. Otherwise they got dusty and all. I remember even whose horses they belonged to was on the trash machine I got here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you have many horses on the place when you were a kid?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: A kid, oh yeah. Horses, yeah. Oh yeah. We always had three horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When were you born?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: 1894. So, by the time you were a kid, he probably had the place pretty well along. Oh yeah, yeah. But I remember the old house when we lived in the old house. It sat way out on the, it was just a few feet from straight down Prenier. But then when they built a new house, they built that further back. So, I don't remember when that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would it take? Do you think it would take them very long to clear up and get the field planted?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: You bet. I remember when we broke a lot of that land. We didn't have no breaking plow. He hired for that breaking. They'd break it and then they had to lay over, lay over till next spring, the brush. And then you took a grub hole and you grubbed out the brush and piled it up and burned it. You had to dig out every brush. It come loose then after it did, you know. If it didn't loose, you can pull it. You had to grub hole and dug it loose. And then you pile it up in big chalks, just like chalks of hay, and you burnt that afterwards. Then you sealed. You have a lot of work. Now, when they break ground now, they got big heavy discs, you know. They go on there and they just cut up the pieces and it's good for the soil. But them days you had to pick everything up because when you come with that spike tooth hair, you know, well that thing clog up with the brush right away. And they just drag the dirt and all. But now the brush don't bother them now at all. But the machinery, heavy machinery just cuts it up into small pieces.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever try your hand at that? Breaking land when you were a kid?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh, I broke land after the first year I was married. I broke land eight acres for a fellow out there on the other side. I was a big buried there. And I broke some land at home too when I was a kid. When I got big enough to break it. But then we had small horses and then we had to go only one way down hill. Otherwise they couldn't pull it. And then of course there was too big a root cell at the chop of the axe. So they go through. If you didn't try to cut off all the root cell, you see they grow again. And you have to have the chair sharp too, otherwise they just slide around the root cell. Some of them roots have a big service, which is as big as that, you know. As big as your wrist. Oh, you bet. I had to cut that with the axe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you seen Troy in the 1901?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, there's some pictures in Ann Driscoll's book that I've seen. But those are the only ones. I was had sister that got married and they lived in that house that's there now. But of course it's been remodeled. They shaver it in there and they hung dynamite back in the trees, you know. Knocked out all the windows for them. It's not like a pretty tough place, Nora. Yeah, they had a saloon there and they were pretty...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you saying that it was quite, that there was really a town there?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yeah, a big store and post office and saloon and black, or blackfish shop and, and barber shop. I get a, well, didn't you see Ann Driscoll… i went with Ann Driscoll, but of course there's nothing there. She didn't get it just right anyway. Here's my half brother, he used to run a trash machine. He's taken on Big Barry again. That's my, my father's on me. There's my father. There's our old team, Champ and Flory. The way they, long time ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have them for a long time?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah. Here's another one of my half brother's trash machine. My dad is there too. There he is there. And this is Fred Tolson, he's still living in Clarkson, in bad shape. He's a, he's a uncle of Russell here. Now this was on Big Bear. Yeah, he's taken on Big Barry. Here's old Dalberg up there, he's from Big Barry. Here's a, there's old man Dalberg now, where Dalberg saw me a lot up there. He used to be Anderson Post Office, he looked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about that Dalberg sawmill? Was that a very big operation?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, he was small, he was small out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are these? Are these just sacks and arms?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: They're sacks over. Oh I see. It's the one sacks. I see this in here. You know this in here hasn't got no, this machine hasn't got no blower on it. It elevates it up. Them days somebody had to be out in the straw stack and take the straw and take it away. But later on they got a blower. These, this in here got a blower, see it blows it up in the big pipe. But this first machine, it didn't have the blower. And at first when you fed them here it had to be two band cutters. You had to, two there to cut the bands and shove it in the machine. What did your father do when he... He pitched bundles, he was a good funnel pitcher.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who saw mill, which saw mill this was?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: The Troy Lumber Company.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh this was the Troy Lumber Company?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, they had saw mills all over years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how would the average saw mill, a saw mill like this work? Work just during the winter for some time or how?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well yeah, they wouldn't, they wouldn't work in the winter time. They log usually in the log in the winter time. Had a bunch of logs there for, so they'd run most of the summer. It hauled it on the sleigh you know. It didn't log in the summer time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of board footage would one of these put out a year? Would they get a lot?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yeah, you bet they'd get a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Say half a million or something like that?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: And they had so many saw mills here and there you know. They had saw mills all over. That was the big office at Troy Lumber Company. And up here at the factory there, they owned this up here in Minkler is now you know. They made everything there. All kinds of that fancy stuff to put on the houses you know. You could get anything there. If they didn't have it, they made it for you. Oh that was the big outfit up here. I worked there a lot in the lumber yard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In town here?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah… in the south of the farm we used to haul lumber for them. Everybody hauled lumber. Some of them hauled year round. That's all they done. Several teams and they hauled lumber for them. They got so much thousand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were they getting most of their lumber from? Was it just off the home places? Off people's farms?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: They bought the timber. A lot of times they bought the land and all. Where there was a lot of timber, they bought the land. And they sold the land after. Good farmland out there you know now. Yeah they had sawmills all over the Troy company. Huh. They owned themselves. Oh yeah you bet. That's the big outfit yet. Troy Company. There's not much trace of them. Clyde got stock in them because his dad had some stock when he passed away. He got some of it. He gets pretty good dividends every year. They got that place in Moscow you know. Troy Company. A yard there. And then they had the big place in Lojian where they made everything too. Just like up here. They tore down all the buildings there and built a big shopping center. They rented out the stores and they make more money that way they say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know who started it up in the first place around here?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: The bowmen… the only bowmen that started the bank on here. They were sawmill metal from Sweden. A bowmen. And then when they got started here then they got some more than Swedes from Sweden. Young fellas, husky workers you know. They worked like slaves you know. Boy they didn't pay much either. They were all good workers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of them settle in around here?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They brought them over all the way from Sweden?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well they said I don't think they paid a fare over. I don't think so but they got them to come over you know. Which most of them come here anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they probably didn't even speak English at first when they came over?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh heck no. I remember when I worked in camp up here in 1928. 21 up back at the Cherry Butte up on Whitmeadows. There was three Swedes come in there then. They couldn't talk any word of English. And they had long tables you know and then at that time they had double decker. All the sweet stuff was on top. And I had to help them pour sweets otherwise they wouldn't get anything to eat. Because they didn't know what to ask for you know. Some would be way down the other end. So I had to help them. Because I could talk Swedish. I still can but it bothers me. That's all the... When I went to school I couldn't talk Swedish or English.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I should say no. That's all you heard around here in Sweden or Norwegian or something. They...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You been up in Canada?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah. You hear all kinds of languages there don't you? I did this summer. So you didn't hear so much English in Troy but it was other languages like... Norwegian, Swedish, Danish or whatever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn very fast when you started school?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well not very fast. It's bad. And then when my wife... That's a long time after that again. My wife was a Petri on them. And she taught school up here on the upper end of Bear Ridge. I think... No that's the second school she had. And she had two kids there that couldn't talk English. She had really a time with them because they were ornery too she said. Stubborn. They couldn't talk a bit of English. And that's quite a few years later than that you know. I don't know why they shouldn't have. But that's all they talked at home you see. And then after a while you know my dad he was off of court in English. He was in Sweden. My mother he talked real good in Sweden. But after we got bigger we never heard anything but English at home. My mother never talked Swedish or Norwegian. She was Norwegian but she talked Swedish. She was raised with the Swedes so she talked clear Swedish. But she never talked... We never talked at home. So my younger brother he don't know how to talk Swedish. But I can talk but it bothers me. I can understand it. When you say bothers you it means... It bothers me to talk to him. Yeah. Because I haven't talked to him for so long. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when you were in school were many of the other kids in the same boat couldn't speak English?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: That's all you heard out on the school ground was Swedish or Norwegian. That's a fact. We'd be out there playing ball or whatever and talk Swedish all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could your teachers speak Swedish?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, no. No. That probably made it harder for her to teach you English. Well she was busy if she was out there and hearing us she would get after us for doing it. But you know she was busy. She had 50 kids and one teacher you know. She was in there working, correcting papers or something. She was hardly ever with us out playing. Well 50 kids in the classroom and one teacher you probably didn't learn too much. I say you didn't. When they called up the class you know they all went in the front seat of the hall. Well I don't remember how many. But if you didn't know the answer by the next one they probably answered or something. You could buy. And that was only three and four months school at that time. And if you were needed at home you were home. It wasn't particularly with the school. So that's the way it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you have for food mostly on the homestead? Food?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: We had plenty to eat. We raised our own. We had chickens and beef and pork, potatoes. Boy we had plenty to eat that's for sure. But boy I remember how hungry we were when we come back from school. We'd go to the pantry and get a slice of bread and we'd put bacon drippings on the bread you know and then sugar on top of that. And boy we used to like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much wild game around?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah. No deer or elk but pheasants and grouse. Grouse oh boy. We used to in the fall of the year when it'd be just before rain they'd come up to the house. If you had the shotgun shoot in the bunch you'd get three or four times. Boy you had nice meat on them. Grouse oh gosh they were plentiful. And rabbits, a lot of rabbits, which there isn't any now. And the grouse they're gone. The pheasants, we had lots of pheasants but I never cared much for them. They tasted vile. There wasn't so much meat on them anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about bear and cougar?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No. Yeah there was cougar. There was cougar but I didn't see any bears. I had a narrow escape with the cougar one time. I went to my aunt's and this was in the winter time about three four feet of snow and cold and in Biddley Hill it must have been thirty below zero. And my sister was laying up there dying. She had TB, a half -sister. She was staying with my aunt. They had a little house for her built out there. So it was up to me to go up and see how she was because no phone them days you know. And it was about four miles up there. And I had to cross that canyon and as I come up the canyon not too far from where we lived. I see there was a black kind of a funny spot on the side of the road there. And the horse I rode he wouldn't go any further. He just started snorting and I started whipping you know. He just reared up on his two legs. So finally I decided I better not try to go by. I figured maybe there is something there. So then there was a... I knew where to go to get up. I had to go up on the ridge and follow the ridge up to the house but there was no trail. So I had to get off the horse. I couldn't ride the horse. There was so much snow. So I had to get off and lead him and the horse had to jump to get through the snow. And so when I got in the house I told my dad about it. And it was awful cold so lots of times people got drunk you know. So dad said, told my brother, he said we better go down and see. So they lift the lantern. They went down there to see and then they seen the tracks and they heard the cougar up above. Then he got on up over the hill. They heard them holler. They holler like a baby you know. So it's a good thing I didn't get off the horse even though they probably grabbed me. And the horse he was scared even. He was snorting even after I got him in the barn. You know they were afraid of animals.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah they used to be cougars but there was one over here by Ava. She was riding horseback and the horse cougars would jump off the tree you know. And all she had on the horse was a surcingle. You know what that is. Just a strap around to hold a blanket on her. Not a saddle. And the horse missed the girl but they grabbed the neck of the horse. But they didn't kill the horse. I guess the horse got away but she fell off. And the horse didn't bother her either but she had a narrow escape there. This was quite a few years ago. Yeah cougars they'll sit in the tree you know. And they'll never, a cougar will never attack you from the front. They got to be in the back. They go like, they grab you from the back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your half sister, she died from tuberculosis?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that pretty serious? The TB in the country?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I see. You bet it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what did they try to do when the person got it? Was there any treatment they tried to save people with?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No they didn't. I had a sister die in Spokane on TB too. The doctor they had there, he treated her for something else on TB. He could have been saved because they had the edge cliff sanitarium up there in Spokane. So by the time they found out she had TB she was too far gone. My brother -in -law was sure mad he wasn't going to pay that bill but I don't know if he ever... He had to pay it anyway. That doctor should have been strung up you know. Because that wasn't necessary for her to die.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was it mostly kids that got it around here?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, no. My half sister there she was about 30 years old. And my sister in Spokane there she was about the same age I guess. She left two kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what about water? Did you have any trouble getting water on the place?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No we had a spring. We had enough off of the spring we couldn't hardly empty it. We had to clean it once in a while you know. We had an awful time to clean it because it come in pretty as that we took it out. We watered all the horses and cattle in there. He's still running too. I was up there about three or four years ago. Spring is still running of course there's no hole he's covered up. Because it was close to the creek you know not far from that little creek there. When the high water come it's filled with dirt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were a kid and you just were fooling around or playing what would you do?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well we made toys we didn't have no bicycle or wagon or nothing. We made stuff out of anything we could. Like wheels out of sleds. We made it the same way. No we never did have a bicycle. And that's too bad if I'd known how to ride a bicycle. I'd have a bicycle now. But I can't ride it. I tried one of Linda's kids here last summer on the Prenu. I felt I could have broke my leg. I said I won't get on one again. It takes a while. Once you get the hang of it you've got it. Yeah I know. It takes a while to get the hang of it. I'm told to start learning things like that now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the winters like out there?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: They were tough. They were really tough. And the mailman come from here you know. He had, well I don't know if you ever went around that way. Well you wouldn't because he went by the old school after we used to go to school. And he had to cross the canyon and go on the other side and then come back again up by Mackenzie's you know. And you know all them hills that'd be drifted. Boy we had to break roads with the horses you know for him to come otherwise he wouldn't come. He couldn't go through. We had to keep on with the horse and drive. And we build up the road you know and then when it gets soft like it is now then you go off. And the horses go off then they'd fall down and then you'd have to dig them out because they were rough about three four feet. Oh they were tough. But we had lots of fun in the wintertime. That's when we had our fun. We had too much crust them days. We'd take a hind bob off Bob's place you know. One of the big slays you know. The hind bob out of that and take it up on the field someplace, turn it loose and boy we'd go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You made a sled out of it?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well yeah I just uncoupled that one hind sled, hind part of the bob you know. Set it on the bunk and just let it go. Because you couldn't guide it you know. Just turn it loose. And then there was a big family on every from 40 to 160 acres. Nobody had over 160 acres. Big families on every place. Some of them had 10, 12 children you know. And on weekends we'd gather there and play hide and seek or something you know. We had the best time. Now there is no kids out in the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The kids used to run together all the time then?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yes. You bet. And then there was dances. They danced but there was only a small room. It was only half as big as this. They'd have dances in there. And they had literary and pie socials.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pie socials?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that? You made pies?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah the women made pies and sold them to raise money for something you know. And basket socials. Then the girls bought them a basket. They'd decorate a basket you know with a lunch in it. And the men would bid on them you know. And then they'd get to eat with the girls. Probably take her home too. Somehow they would really bid on them if they knew which girl it was they had in the basket. Boy they bid them up. And then we had horses. I had, as soon as I was 15 years old, I had to be my horse and cut her. And go and with lots of bills on them. We'd go cut her riding in the wintertime. Take my girl off you know. No we had lots of fun. We had lots more fun in the fairs than the kids have now. I think you're probably right because it seems like these days... ...when they're gone. Them days you didn't have the car. You walked to your neighbors. And of course if you knew anybody else that was going there, they'd all flock to one place and we'd all be together and have a good time.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: What it sounds like you're saying is there was a heck of a lot more visiting back and forth with the families too. More Deary. I say almost every Sunday we'd be invited someplace or we'd have more someplace. And I remember sometimes it got come up a storm. There was up close to Deary we stayed up there one night. Made a bid on the floor. We stayed the morning. After it rained like this you know, you've been hauling on that road and it just turned to ice. And if you didn't have shoes on or horses you were pretty stuck. So we stayed that night I remember the morning. The cows had a way to get milk the morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you stay? Somebody's house?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah. We stayed at the family with it. Rather than go home because I rode the ice even in Deary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of the hauling done in the winters?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: All of it. Well I wouldn't say all of it. There was a few here in Troy. That's all they done but haul you know. And they had to haul year round. They had a wagon. But most of the hauling was done in the wintertime. Most of them had cordwood you know. Take all the around north of Troy here that's all they lived on. They made wood in the wintertime and bumped it out someplace where they get all of it and haul in the wintertime. And that's the way it was with most of the lumber too that was hauling in the wintertime. You want to see the lumber yard they had up here. Lumber no matter where you look. Up here where the tarmac is now. All that lumber pile. And on the upper side of the road below the schoolhouse there was lumber on both sides. It's an alien pile on both sides. I didn't know that this was such a big lumber town. I'll tell you, you wouldn't believe it. They used to call this the biggest town on earth. And I'll tell you it really was. You come in here on Saturday, you would have an awful time to find a place to tie your horses. There'd be horses and wagons or sleds no matter where you look. Everything was hauled into Troy. Wood, apples, grain, cordwood. Dusty down here run the warehouse he bought everything. No other boy ever said this he'd wait. There was three saloons here and two banks, two or three hardware stores. Oh, this was quite a town I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you haul in wood from as far out as your place?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, we didn't. Well, I hauled probably a cord or two, that's all. No, I didn't like that cordwood. It was hard work splitting the wood. But a lot of people, that's all they done. I know some of them, that's all they done was to make cordwood, or dollar cord. And boy, I don't see how they can make enough to live on what they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be pretty far for you to come from all the way out there in the town with all the wood? You know how much you got for a cord of wood here if they're dry?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Three and a half and four dollars a cord. So you can imagine. And unless you had an awful big team you couldn't haul over a cord for a small team, a cord and a half for the big team. So now you couldn't, a fella wouldn't make a potential cord now. It sounds like the further out you'd go, the harder it'd be to make a living, bring it in to Troy. Yeah, it's good, but they had to, that's all the living they had. That's what they lived on. And of course everybody on the farm, he had something to sell when he went to town. He had butter, eggs, potatoes, all this stuff to bring in. Now they had nothing to bring in. The farmers, they come in to buy there and take it out the farm. No, that's what's wrong with the country. You know that they're going to have to go back to the farm, this here will never work out. Them big farmers taking over now and now you can't get back. Machinery is too high and you can't buy the farm, they're too high. It'll never work out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think it's going to go back to the old ways sometimes?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: They've got to go back to the old ways. I see now where they're going to, in Lewiston they're going to have, I guess the government is going to furnish the seed for garden, rent the plants so they can, a bunch of them go together and raise their own garden. They're going to have to do something like that. That's what's the matter with the country now. Farmers haven't got a cow, they haven't got a chicken, they buy everything here. If they raised everything they needed on the farm, of course there isn't many yachts on the farm either, that's the worst of them. Things wouldn't be so high here in the store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Talk about Troy and it being a real lively town, it makes me think about the toughness of Troy. They say it was a pretty tough town in the early days. Well, I think most of them were the Sullivans and Sullivans and heavy drinking drinkers. You know, when a person gets drunk most of them don't know what to do. They get mean to them. I don't know if it was any tougher than the other town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you know about old Hayes, that marshal in town?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I don't know much about him. I just remember that all. I was pretty young. And then, you know, we lived out there nine miles out of here, so somebody my age that lived here in Troy, he would know more about it. Because we must have been here, we didn't come in here every day until nine miles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About how often would you come in here?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Once a week at least. And of course then we had closure up to Anderson. That was five miles away. We had to go up there to get a mail anyway, so me and my brother used to go up there about twice a week to get a mail. Then we'd fetch some eggs or something to get a little coffee or sugar or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well now, by Anderson, it was near there that they had that water power sawmill, right?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, that was down below. That's down about four miles below. Yeah,… i hauled logs to that sawmill. I never worked at the sawmill, but I hauled logs there. I hauled logs twice. Two winters down to that mill. Three winters, by golly. They used to take in part of it and log there. They'd have about three or four months run there every summer, spring. Now they couldn't run one month, I don't think. Because there wouldn't be enough water. Just when the high water is, that's all. So it changed quite a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Joe Wells at all?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yes, you bet. I knew all them Wellses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about Joe?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, he was a pretty nice fellow, except when he got drunk he got kind of a little bit, I don't know what I should say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mean?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, he wasn't exactly mean. No, he wasn't mean. He was quite a logger, he logged. He logged around the area a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you know about Joe and his wife?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: You know, they were raised with the white people. Grew up with the white people. Well, Joe didn't. Joe was a slave. Joe and his wife, they were slaves. Wells bought them during the slavery. And then when the slaves got free, Joe and his wife liked the people, so they stayed with them. The people that they stayed with, they were blacksmiths. They liked the white people because they took good care of them. They didn't work them too hard or anything. You can imagine when they took their name, even, why they must have liked them. I heard he got along, he was real close to the Wells boys that were out here, the other Wells. Oh yeah, sure, they were just like brothers. They moved away from Derry and they moved over here to Buffalo Hill, you know, where the Buffaloes are and all. That place on this side, that was the Wells brothers. They died there. I heard they did well for themselves in Derry. I heard they did well in Derry with blacksmithing. Oh yeah, yeah. Then they had, Joe and his wife had three children, Chuck and Roy and Mary. And then Mary had, well, Mary had, have you got that going?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I don't want to say anything then. Anyway, because I've known him all my life and I used to go and see him when he was in the nursing home up here. When he passed away I went to the funeral. So in about a week I had a letter from Louis Drew. Thank me. He got drunk. He'd get out there on the street and he'd say, I'm the only white man in Derry, he says, all the rest of them are Swedes. Yeah, he said that though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear of him having any run -ins with Hayes?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I've heard about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear of him having run -ins with Hayes?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, no, I never did. No. This Hayes, I'm just curious about what you, if you heard that he was, see I've heard he was unpopular. People didn't like him very much. No, I know. I know he was unpopular. There was a woman here, she was related to flies, I guess. And she was going to write a book about this Hayes. She stayed around a couple of days, I guess, trying to find out about everything here and she took pictures of where he used to be and the place where he got killed up there on the side hill there. But I never heard nothing more about it. I don't think she ever done anything about it. I never heard of it. But there is some flies that I believe in Clarkson or I think in the Lewis and Dubuque, fly is that name. I think it's part of them yet, the younger generation of course. I knew the brother of that fly. He had a labor bar in here at one time. But the other fellow I didn't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear about how he got killed?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: How Hayes got... Well, I heard about it and I forget how it was. I couldn't say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about Driscoll stabbing him, getting killed? Driscoll?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Driscoll, he got killed, he got stabbed by a shoemaker. No. That was another one of the... Well, I've heard that as an early day, Troy. I never heard of it. There was an awful lot of... How they ever, you know, did get drunk here with four horse teams and they drive home. I don't know how they made it, but they made it. You know, that's why we have such a wide street here because they wanted... It's wide enough so they could turn around with a four horse team on the street here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that why?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah. And I imagine that's it because they couldn't afford a seat and there's going to be a lot of traffic, you know. So the fellow that laid it out, he wanted to have it wide enough so he could turn around any place with a four horse team. And you can do that here. With this drinking, though, was it just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was mostly just lumberjacks from around the country or was it mostly local men?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Farmers, farmers. A lot of them farmers out here, heavy drinkers. They were then. Up here in Nora, almost all of us drinkers, drunkards, they all drank heavy. But they made some of them as well to do, too, besides, even if they drank. But they got drunk pretty often. Yeah, they were heavy drinkers up in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember Nora being like when you were over there as a kid, I mean, besides the stores and stuff?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, you know, there were some women who drank them days, too, but not very many. I remember some women who went into the saloon, but they never had very good reputation if you walk in there now. But now anybody walking there now, their reputation seems to be good. But there were a few that drank them days, too. Not too many. My dad, he never drank. He was quite a religious man. But whenever we went to Troy here, he'd go in, he'd have one of them half a gallon lard pails, you know. Go into the saloon, he'd buy a pail of that for 15 cents. He'd get a half a gallon for 15 cents. And then he'd buy a box of crackers and some cheese, and that's what we ate. And I liked it, too. That really went good. Cheese and crackers and then a glass of beer. Just think of that, 15 cents for a half a gallon.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I can't believe it. Well, years ago you went to a saloon and bought a glass of beer and you got almost a meal to go with it. And the way it was in every saloon, they put that out there and you could get 15 cents, I think. So there were lots of people, that's all they lived on. I've heard about it in Spokane. They used to have those smorgasbords, the spreads out there for free if you bought a schooner of beer. Yeah, they had it all over in the saloon. And Schleiber cheese, I don't know. I don't think that was very much, 10, 15 cents for that, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start working in the woods?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh, I started pretty young. About 15, 16 years old, I guess. I started working for my uncle up close by, there either. He was hauling logs for the War of Harville and I used to help him cut down the trees and skid them. And then when I got a little bigger, well then he'd haul logs for the pot butter with the pancake. Then I had a team over there and he hauled and I was supposed to deck him while he was gone. He'd dump his load. Then you put skids on, two skids up to the skidway, you know, like that, to the ground. Then you had to have a cable. You go around the log, along the log and then hook it up here and you support. Then the horses pull that log up, they roll them up there on the skids. But you had to send them just right. Otherwise they'd go this way or this way, you know. You had to cut them on the go so they'd go up straight. Boy, I had lots of close calls here, nothing but the kids, they wanted to get killed. It was Daniel's job, you know, sometimes they come down and they probably call me sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you be doing? What was your job on that?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I was decking, that's what they call decking logs. And you're supposed to cut them on the, when they're gone, you know. They're easy to cut but they can't help when they're traveling. Otherwise you can't cut them. They cut easy one way or the other so they go up straight. So you'd be trying to direct them while they were... I put them up on the skid with them while he was getting another load.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You used a cant hook?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah. A lot of people don't know what a cant hook is anymore. In a peavey, that's another thing, you can pry loose, you know. There's a sharp edge on the end. A can't hook hasn't got that, it just, that's for the cut logs. Well I've done everything in the woods. Skidding on the high lines or ground lines. I worked around lots of donkeys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that what happened then? You just started working in the woods from there?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, that's what started me at Lumberjack from there on. I wasn't satisfied with that being old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you go next?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well I started with the Potlatch then, from there. I drove team for them most of the time I drove. Team for bringing 25 years for them skidded logs. Then I worked on the high lines and ground lines and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did they, do you remember when you first started driving the team for them? When they first put you on it? How that happened?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, no, I went to camp to hire up for the teamster. That's what I wanted to do, I didn't want to do anything else but drive a team. I never did like to saw logs, although I done it some. That's one thing I didn't like was sawing logs. So I always applied for a team if I get one. And usually you did get the team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you work? Where did they have you work first when you were driving team? Whereabouts?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, I think the first, when I drove team, that's from the time when you just held the lines, you couldn't touch anything else. You were driving team then, you didn't do nothing else. And I think that was on a bear kick at horse's camp. The Potlatch couldn't buy the timber so the fellow named my horse, he had a bank in Derry. They got him to dicker with the farmers, so he got the timber. So they called it the horse's camp, it was Potlatch. And then they sent a whole bunch of men out in the woods and they'd make a road to every tree, slash a road to every tree, and limb the logs. They even had a cantor or a PV and rolled the logs over so there were no limbs on the logs at all, no place underneath either. And then when the teamsters come along, you drive up there and you have a man along and he grabs a hold of the tongs. Tongs are a fastener on the double trees. And he grabs a hold of them then as the team turned around and he hooks it onto the log. Or if there's more than one log, you put chain. In fact, you have four logs in a chain, it's a small one. But if it's a big log, you have a tong, you know, they grab him like this, they hold the log. Yeah, either side of us. So, them days, when you drove a team, that's all you did, you hang on the line, you didn't do nothing else. But they soon got away with that. And then the jip won't start. So, then you had to do everything yourself. There was a man in a swamper then. The teamster, he drove the horses and helped limb or whatever to get the logs out. But the swamper, he was supposed to make the road in there and cut the limbs off. But lots of times he couldn't keep up, you know, and then you helped him. Then you were two men to the job, you see. You got so much a thousand. That's what was getting him in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which way was better?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, it was better the other way because it made more work for the men. And of course you made a little more money working by your jip own, but you worked hard. They found out how much you could do then, that's what rooted. Before, they took ten men to do one man's job, see. After the jip owned, they found out that one man could do ten men's job. And then you were stuck. And then they started cutting the wages too. They made big money to start with because they didn't know how much you could do, you see. But then they tore in and worked hard, you know, to make a lot of money. And then they made too much money, the company wouldn't stand for it. They cut the wages, cut them down. And that's the way they kept on, cutting them down because the men was working too hard. So it was better the other way because it made work for a lot of people. They used to call the Potlatch, they'd owe people's homes. Because them old fellas, they could go out there and make roads or trim trees. There's a lot of things they could do, you know, monkey around. They didn't have to work hard. They were on their own out there working, making roads and trimming. So they really had a nice. So you think that really that the wage, working wages was better for the lumberjack and the whole. It was better because that contract work should never have been. That fella started that, he should have strung him up right away, get rid of him. Because that was the real nation to the country. You take out in the camp, no, you go out there and afterwards, you know, after I got on the cab, I'll tell you. Well, we, they put three men with the cat. I was the rigging man, then I had two shokers. Well, I had to say look for a rope to get into the timber. And tell the, my rigging man where to go to get in. And then I, then after they got the shokers on all the logs, then I hooked them shokers on the bullhook on the cat, see. That was my job. Well, pretty soon, pretty soon they cut down on them, so they got only one man with the cat. And the cat driver had to go off and help set shokers. That's the way it is now. They working for the cat driver and the shokers that are working together now. And that's against the law for any, for cat drivers to leave the cat. Because you never know when he's gonna, and one man lost his leg because the breaks that go when he was working behind him got on his leg and he had to cut it off. It is against the law, but they do it. You still work it that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you feel about changing over to cats from horses?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I didn't like it a bit. I thought sure I was gonna, I thought I was gonna quit. But I've been working for so long too, you know, there wasn't very many jobs I could take because I, that's all I brought new farming and logging, so. But they worked me in on the cat, slowly. And pretty soon I got to a light. I didn't like it at first because we didn't have no A -frame in front there. We just had a cat, you had to, if there was a log, you had to haul the logs out, cut them and haul them out so we could get through. But pretty soon they gave us an A -frame. We let that down and pushed everything out of sight and you made a road as you went. Then I liked it pretty good. Because all I had to do was tell the cat driver where to go and he went in there and I had the road. Huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Axel Anderson?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I ought to know him. I worked for him for years. I worked for him for years. Good old scout. Yeah, I'd always, whenever he opened his camp he'd always call me to come go to work… yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he encourage you to work on cats instead of?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well, he wanted me, I broke my ankle up there with Bob Clark one time, so I wasn't supposed to work in the woods anymore. So they gave me a patrol. I, uh, I graded roads and watered the roads for the truck, you see. That was my job. So I had the road in pretty good shape and so there was some cat sitting along the road and he'd come along sometimes. He said, say, he says, why don't you get on one of them cats, he said, and drive him. I'd like to see you start driving one of them cats. And I said, well, Axel, I said, I'm too old to start one of them. Ah, he said, there's fellas here with long whiskers, I said, he said, driving cats. Yeah, I said, but they don't know how to drive it either. I told him, I said, you've got to start young on them. I knew I wouldn't be no good cat driver. No, I didn't want to start driving cats.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: But, uh, he thought I didn't have enough to do, I guess I, uh, the road was in pretty good shape. So I could sit around waiting and, uh, waiting for the trucks to go by. The idea I've had about Axel is that he expected a lot from his men, but he was pretty fair to them. Oh, he was, uh, well, he wanted you to work, sure, but he was a pretty good cat. But he was pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did it make a big difference in how good the foreman was that you worked for?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: You bet it did. You bet. And a fellow like that, he always kept a pretty good crew, too. Because, uh, lumberjacks, they were pretty touchy and all. They didn't like the boss, they didn't. Well, of course, he was, uh, he probably was hard on some of them. Thought they were, but they couldn't. You know, there's men coming into camp, they take, they can do anything, and they couldn't do nothing. They had teamsters come in there, they put the collar upside down on the horse. They didn't know any better, and they wanted to drive team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What can a boss do with anything like that?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Can't do nothing but send them down the road. You can't have the collar upside down on the horse, they got it over you. And one time he got a fellow from Kendrick, I remember he, he was skidding along not far from where I was. He hollered to beat everything on his horses, just like a sheep herder, you know. And all acts would come along, and, oh, he said, take a team to the barn, he said, you better go herd sheep, he said. That was the last I ever heard. I get the idea there was a lot of, a lot of new faces in the woods all the time, people coming and going. Oh yeah, yes, lots of new faces. A lot of people come in every day for a while looking to work, you know. Some of them just travel to camp to another, looking for a good job. And then they get a free meal, free lodging.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think I've heard those guys, do they used to call those guys camp inspectors?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, in, I'll tell you, in 1929, let's see, yes, winter of 29, I worked out of here on the other side of Helmer, camp 7. There was one fellow there, he went with us to lunch for, lunch room, and made a lunch every morning. Took a big lunch and he went out to some cab and stayed all day and come back the next night and slept there. He kept on that way for a long time. He finally caught up with them and from there on, then they'd clamp down on coming in there and taking lunch out. You know, we had it nice there. We used to be able to go in there and take a pound or two of coffee, eggs, about a dozen, big slab of bacon. And anything you butter by the pound, we'd take it out the woods and then we'd cook our dinner there, you see. Most of the time we'd get frying pan, if they didn't have enough frying pan, we used our shovel, cooked shovel, you see. You can fry pretty good on them or a fire. So we really had a nice, boy we had good meals there. Ham and bacon and eggs, you know. Boy. But they had to cut down on them because a lot of people come in there and they took it and they went.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: And this fellow probably had somebody else staying with him too and he just brought the food. A lot of them weren't working, you know, they just kept on doing that. So I think most of the time they charged for meals, but of course they never charged me, I was too well known anyway. They knew I wouldn't do that. I always got a meal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the chuck like in the camps?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Always good. Everything. All kinds of meat and steak and cookies and cake and pies for every meal, even breakfast. Everything is loaded with food, you can't imagine. But still a lot of people came, a lot of people kicked on the meal. Well the lumberjacks used to say you want a cake no matter how good it is. And that's what they did. No, they were really fed up, boy. Hot cakes in the morning, you got hot ones too. If it wasn't hot they'd put them in the stove and come in and get another plate for it. No, that was good food in camp, that's for sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the scaling like? What was the scaling like?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, I've heard that it was pretty tough a lot of times to get your scale right. Well I know the scalers had to steal so much every day otherwise they don't last the job. It had to be so much over. That's the way they had to figure it out. I heard that they had to figure out that it was going to pay for the song of the mill, for the overrun. I know it was an awful overrun, that's for sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean overrun?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well they didn't give quite a lot in the log at the museum.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It probably should have been up to the next number but it did go back once, see?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: And that amounted quite a bit on a day's work. But the scalers couldn't help it because they were hired to do that. And if they didn't scale that way they went down the road because they had a check -scaler come. They never knew when the check -scaler would check on them, see? Check -scaler was in every week and maybe twice a week they came and checked on them, on the scalers. Eddie Anderson down there in the beer parlor used to be a scaler. Down here in this lower beer parlor, you know? He was pretty well liked in camp though. I guess he gave a good to the scalers he was there to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that commonly known? Did most of the men just know they were getting cheated?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh yeah, they all knew that. They all knew that. Sometimes they'd shield them in the office too if the scale come in and they had too much or they'd fix it up there so they wouldn't get too much if you thought you made too much money. I know that went on too. See the bosses, they were afraid of higher ups too because they let the men make too much money. In 1921 -22 when they first started gyppoing there, they started gyppoing, they didn't even have a swamper. They worked alone, you know. And they make the big money, they bought big cars. Gee, they had big cars and all around the camp there. It wasn't long until the higher ups caught up to that and boy, they clamped down on them and pretty soon they didn't have no car. They didn't make enough to run to that big car. Oh, they made big money there for a while. But you see, the working man, he was always a fool. If he'd worked slower and not made that big money, kept it down, worked a little easier, he could have kept on having good wages. But instead of that, he wanted to make more on the bosses and they wouldn't stand for that.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: And that's when they found out how much a man could do and that was Ruination.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did some former try to help the men instead of help the company?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: No, I don't think there were very many of them. There was a few, I know one up there. He was, Ed Carson and I, we took a job making, we was, the logging shut down one winter up there. And so we stayed in camp, we lived in camp anyway. So we stayed there that winter and then my partner, he wanted to be ambitious so he took a job making poles for the potlatch. So I was supposed to help, we took that job. But we didn't make much money, but I guess he didn't make no difference, we didn't have anything else to do, but we wasn't satisfied. And the boss, he felt sorry for us. So he told us, he said when he come out to the visitor, boy he said, I feel sorry for you fellas. He said, you're not making no money. No, we said we knew that. Well, he said, I'll leave the door open into the, where they kept all the food, you know. He said, you go in there and help yourself, which is what you need. But we never did.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: So, well he liked to have us there too because him and his wife, they could go on a trip and we still looked after the camp. He got paid for staying there and looking after the camp. But we lived there so when he was gone we looked after the camp. So when we didn't go in and get anything, he gave us some stuff, he gave us some bacon, a few gallons of fruit and so on. That's really considerate. That sounds good. Yeah, he was a really nice knife guy. He didn't live very long either. Either him or her, he died pretty young. But there wasn't very many bosses. They were used to having all kinds of men. They were, I don't think they felt sorry for anybody much. They had all kinds of men to deal with, you know. Some were pretty hard to deal with too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those guys, most of the guys that were transient lumberjacks just coming through? Just there for a short time?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, they weren't cranky coming through because they always act nice then but when they got stinky that's when they got cranky. But that's the time when the camp boss would let them go to town and blow in. Then they were real nice when they come back. They worked like everything and they were easy to get along with. So the boss always let them go and go to town and blow in because it didn't take more than a day or two, you know. They were broke.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what's the deal on that? What happened? A lumberjack could take all his earnings and just go and blow them all right away?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Well they had a woman in every town. I heard one fellow that used the barn balls. I don't know if this was true or not. But he said he had her in Seattle so he went to Seattle. And he came to the door and knocked on the door and I guess she had somebody else in there so he stuck the check under the door for her. But now I don't know. That don't seem like anybody would do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He'd want more than that for the money, wouldn't he?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: But you know they all had a chippy some place. They all had a woman some place. And then they get drunk and they'd be broke in a little while. Like crying gust up there every time. It wasn't taken. He had a four or five hundred dollar to go to town. He broke right away. Then he cried. So they called him crying gust.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He cried when his money was gone?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, he cried every time.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: That's funny.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: You know like him now, he didn't give nothing for his money. I, when we lived in Elk River, we was going to Moscow so he asked me if I could go along. Well I said yeah, you can go along. But I was kind of leery about him because I know he was crazy. When he come to town he wanted to buy my wife everything. Hats and dresses and everything. My wife wouldn't take it. But as we walked down the street, you know, I met somebody and the girl smiled at him. And by golly he pulled out a ten dollar giver just for smiling at her. That's the way kind of a guy he was. And he said, well, you see his money went. Well he didn't get broke when, I brought him back to Elk River, but he got broke that night in Elk River. How he could get broke, I don't know. Some woman took all the money away from him. What I can't figure out is, well I've heard this before, that the men just blew their, blew in, blew their stake and it was all gone. But I still can't quite figure out why they had to spend all their money instead of putting some of it away to save it. No, no, that's the way they were. And there was one fellow, a noble fellow up there, he used to be a black, his name was Johnson. He had his family in Sweden. He come out here to make a stake and now he's going to go back, you see. And sometimes he get a letter from one of the kids in Sweden, you know, and his tears were coming to his eyes when he read them letters, you know. Then he tell me afterwards, he said, now I'm going to save my money, he said, I'm going back to Sweden now this fall. And you know he never got there, he died, he died here. And that's the way it was most time. And they never got any place because as soon as they get to town, why they were broke. He just couldn't, couldn't get by the town without going in there, get drunk and blow it out. No matter how they got their intentions yet.

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: I take it there was a big difference between the lumberjack that was a single man and the lumberjack that was a family man and lived around here. Oh yeah, yeah, that was a big difference. But you know when they come right down to it, I was surprised. A lot of them old lumberjacks been married one time and had children some place too. But they just got into lumber camps and they forgot all about them. No, I was surprised. Lots of times I never thought that anybody, that he'd ever been married but yeah, I found out he was married, had been married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the lumberjacks do in camp? What was the camp life like when they weren't out working?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: They'd sit there and bullshit or play cards or something. They'd tell big stories. A lot of them, you don't know if you believe it or not. So I'd come in there and brag about what all they could do and what they had been doing. I always found out a fella that bragged like that couldn't do nothing. But I want to look out for a man like that come in and brag about what he could do. By gosh, he couldn't do nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you ever know Dick Farrell?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Yeah, you bet. I used to go to all his meetings. He was a nice guy. All the lumberjacks was glad to see him come because he traveled from camp to camp. He knew all the lumberjacks and did the fine art where this and that fella was. So they were all glad to see him. Yeah, he packed a pack sack on his back like the lumberjacks. Slept with them in the camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what his preaching was like?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh, he's a good preacher. Preach as good as any other preacher. Yeah, he was a good man. I heard that he'd been a prize fighter. Yeah, he was. He was a pretty tough man, too. That's what he was. No prize fighter. He got religion. He quit it. He thought he'd go. Because he'd been with the rough guys, he thought he'd go with the rough ones in the camp to see him. Try to save some of them, but I don't think he ever saved any of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was he trying to get the guys to quit drinking?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh, yeah, sure… and you know, after a man had been drunk in camp, one of them real drunkards, when they come to the table, then the next time they'd empty every bottle, they'd cut the bottles or whatever, anything in the bottle, they'd drink that. They'd drink anything. Some of them drank hair tonic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Speaking of drinking, do you remember Pat Malone?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, if there ever was a bottle around, he'd be there. You're a pretty good old scout. Yeah, he used to be at all the dairy dances. He'd be out with the boys, and the boys would go out. He knew there would be a bottle, why, he'd get a drink. Yeah, he was a nice guy. He tried to keep order though, but he liked his whiskey. They have a lot of funny things about him, and that's the greed he treats, and I guess they're so worried. Yeah, he was well known, old Pat was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the IWWs, these Wobblies?

speaker: Philip Asplund
words: With a few straws in the bottom,… and this was up above Lowell at Perdue then, when I was working there, at Collins, there was a saloon that was about a mile and a half up above where I worked the saloon. So anytime in the night there towards morning, they'd be coming drunk, drunk from the bunkhouse the whole time. If you were alone in the bunkhouse, you'd roll in with it. And then there was ten hour days in the fall of the year, you had to go out there and stand there, build a fire, and stand by the fire an hour before you go to work, because it was too dark to see, but you had to be out there. And another thing, there was no place to wash your clothes. All you did, they bought kerosene in them five gallon kerosene cans, you know, you've seen them, as you... Well, they opened them up on top, and then they went down to the creek and built a fire underneath them, and got hot water, put a little soap in there, and got one of them things to push up and down, wash your clothes in there. That's what they had to do on Sundays. No place to wash your clothes, only then. So, you know, that wasn't very good. But after that, if they got straightened out to the bunkhouse and everything, there'd be good beds and blankets and the whole way. And then they had to wash their houses and that house and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And these Wobblies, the Wobblies helped to bring that in?

speaker: Philip Asplund
